title: 7 Defensive Stocks That Perform Well in Market Downturns
metaDescription: 7 Defensive Stocks That Perform Well in Market Downturns. Discover recession-resistant companies with strong fundamentals, reliable dividends, and a history of outperforming during bear markets.
excerpt: Discover 7 defensive stocks that have historically weathered market downturns with resilience. Each pick includes in-depth analysis of why these companies thrive when the broader market struggles.

content.introduction: When markets enter a downturn, the natural instinct for many investors is to sell everything and head for the exits. However, history has shown time and again that bear markets and recessions are not the time to abandon stocks entirely — they are the time to own the right stocks. Defensive stocks, those belonging to companies that provide essential goods and services people need regardless of the economic environment, have a proven track record of preserving capital and even generating positive returns when the broader market is declining. These companies tend to operate in sectors like consumer staples, healthcare, utilities, and discount retail, where demand remains relatively constant because their products and services are non-negotiable aspects of daily life. During the 2008 financial crisis, the S&P 500 lost nearly 38% of its value, yet many defensive stocks either limited losses to single digits or actually posted gains. During the 2020 COVID-19 crash, consumer staples and healthcare stocks recovered far more quickly than cyclical sectors like travel and energy. The key to defensive investing is understanding that these companies share common traits: strong balance sheets with manageable debt levels, consistent cash flows that support and grow dividends, dominant market positions that are difficult to displace, and business models that are inherently resistant to economic cycles. While defensive stocks may underperform during roaring bull markets, their value becomes unmistakable when the tide turns. This article examines seven defensive stocks that have demonstrated remarkable resilience through multiple market downturns and are well-positioned to protect your portfolio whenever the next recession arrives.

items.name: Walmart (WMT)
items.detail: Walmart is arguably the single most recession-resistant company in the world, and its stock has proven this repeatedly through multiple downturns. The logic is straightforward: when household budgets tighten, consumers trade down from premium retailers and specialty stores to Walmart's everyday low prices. During the 2008-2009 financial crisis, while most retailers saw dramatic revenue declines, Walmart's same-store sales actually increased as middle-class shoppers flocked to its stores for groceries and household essentials. Grocery sales now account for approximately 60% of Walmart's U.S. revenue, and food is about as non-discretionary as consumer spending gets — people must eat regardless of what the stock market or economy is doing. Beyond its core retail business, Walmart has been aggressively expanding its e-commerce capabilities, Walmart+ membership program, and advertising business, creating new high-margin revenue streams that supplement its massive physical store footprint. The company operates over 10,500 stores across 19 countries, giving it unparalleled scale and purchasing power that allows it to undercut competitors on price. Its supply chain efficiency is legendary in the retail industry, and this logistical advantage becomes even more valuable during economic slowdowns when cost-conscious consumers comparison-shop more aggressively. Walmart has also increased its dividend for over 50 consecutive years, making it a Dividend King and providing investors with a growing income stream even during the darkest economic periods. For portfolio protection during downturns, Walmart's combination of essential products, value pricing, and growing digital capabilities makes it an indispensable holding.

items.name: Johnson & Johnson (JNJ)
items.detail: Johnson & Johnson is a healthcare colossus that has been a cornerstone of defensive portfolios for decades, and for good reason. Healthcare spending is among the most non-discretionary of all consumer expenditures — people do not stop taking their medications, undergoing necessary surgeries, or using medical devices simply because the economy has entered a recession. JNJ operates across three segments — pharmaceuticals, medical devices, and consumer health — providing diversified revenue streams that reduce dependence on any single product or market. The pharmaceutical division, which now accounts for the majority of revenue following the Kenvue consumer health spinoff, includes blockbuster drugs like Stelara, Tremfya, and Darzalex that treat chronic conditions requiring ongoing treatment regardless of economic conditions. The medical technology segment provides essential surgical and orthopedic equipment used in procedures that cannot be indefinitely postponed. What truly sets JNJ apart is its financial fortress: it is one of only a handful of U.S. companies to hold a AAA credit rating, even higher than the U.S. government's rating. This means JNJ can borrow at the lowest possible rates, giving it enormous financial flexibility during credit crunches when other companies struggle to access capital. The company has increased its dividend for 62 consecutive years, another Dividend King designation that demonstrates management's unwavering commitment to returning value to shareholders through all market conditions. During the 2008 crisis, JNJ stock declined less than 8% while the S&P 500 fell nearly 38%, and it quickly recovered to new highs while the broader market took years to rebound.

items.name: Procter & Gamble (PG)
items.detail: Procter & Gamble is the quintessential defensive stock, manufacturing and selling the everyday household products that consumers continue to purchase in virtually any economic environment. Its portfolio includes some of the most recognized and trusted brands in the world — Tide laundry detergent, Crest toothpaste, Gillette razors, Pampers diapers, Charmin toilet paper, and Bounty paper towels, among dozens of others. These are not products that consumers can easily eliminate from their budgets when times get tough; they represent essential spending on personal hygiene, household cleaning, and basic care. During economic downturns, P&G actually benefits from a phenomenon known as the "lipstick effect," where consumers may trade down from luxury goods but continue spending on small, affordable indulgences like premium personal care products. P&G's brand loyalty is extraordinary — many of its products have maintained dominant market share positions for decades, and consumers are reluctant to switch to generic alternatives even when budgets tighten, because the price difference on a tube of toothpaste or a bottle of shampoo is relatively small compared to the perceived quality difference. The company has raised its dividend for 68 consecutive years, the longest streak of any company on this list, making it the ultimate Dividend King. This unbroken record of dividend increases spans multiple recessions, oil crises, inflationary periods, and financial panics. P&G's operating margins consistently exceed 20%, reflecting the pricing power that comes with owning category-defining brands. Its global footprint — products sold in over 180 countries — provides geographic diversification that further insulates the company from any single region's economic difficulties.

items.name: Coca-Cola (KO)
items.detail: Coca-Cola is one of the most recognizable brands on the planet, and its stock has served as a defensive haven through generations of market turmoil. The company sells beverages in more than 200 countries and territories, with a distribution network so extensive that it has been called the most pervasive brand in human history. This global reach provides remarkable diversification — a recession in one region is typically offset by stable or growing demand elsewhere. Coca-Cola's product portfolio has evolved well beyond its namesake soda to include water, sports drinks, coffee, tea, and juice brands, reducing its dependence on any single category and addressing the long-term consumer trend toward healthier beverage choices. During downturns, Coca-Cola benefits from the fact that its products are affordable luxuries — a bottle of Coke or Dasani water costs far less than most discretionary purchases and provides a small daily pleasure that consumers are reluctant to give up. The company's franchise bottling model means it does not carry the heavy capital expenditure of manufacturing and distribution, resulting in high margins and substantial free cash flow generation. Coca-Cola has increased its dividend for 62 consecutive years, placing it firmly in Dividend King territory. Warren Buffett's Berkshire Hathaway is the company's largest shareholder, a position it has held since 1988, which serves as perhaps the most powerful endorsement of Coca-Cola's enduring competitive advantages. During the 2008 financial crisis, Coca-Cola's stock fell less than 15% while generating a dividend yield above 3%, allowing patient investors to earn meaningful income while waiting for the recovery.

items.name: Duke Energy (DUK)
items.detail: Utilities are among the most classic defensive investments, and Duke Energy stands as one of the largest and most reliable utility companies in the United States. Duke Energy provides electricity to approximately 8.2 million customers across North Carolina, South Carolina, Florida, Indiana, Ohio, and Kentucky, and natural gas to an additional 1.6 million customers. The fundamental defensive case for utilities is simple and compelling: people and businesses need electricity and gas regardless of what the stock market is doing or whether the economy is growing or contracting. Demand for electricity is extraordinarily stable — lights stay on, heating and cooling systems run, and appliances continue to operate through recessions and expansions alike. Duke Energy operates as a regulated utility in most of its service territories, meaning its rates and allowed returns are set by public utility commissions. While this regulation limits upside during boom times, it also provides a guaranteed return on invested capital that is insulated from competitive pressures and economic cycles. The regulated model effectively creates a legal moat around the business, as competitors cannot enter Duke's service areas without regulatory approval. Duke Energy has been investing heavily in clean energy transition, retiring coal plants and building solar, wind, and battery storage capacity, which positions it well for the energy transition while also creating opportunities for rate-base growth that supports future earnings expansion. The stock typically yields between 3.5% and 4.5%, making it an attractive income vehicle during downturns when bond yields may be declining. During the 2008 crisis, utility stocks as a group fell less than half as much as the S&P 500, and Duke Energy specifically declined only about 12% while continuing to pay its dividend uninterrupted.

items.name: AbbVie (ABBV)
items.detail: AbbVie is a pharmaceutical powerhouse that has established itself as one of the most resilient healthcare stocks in the market, combining strong defensive characteristics with impressive growth potential. Spun off from Abbott Laboratories in 2013, AbbVie built its early success on Humira, which became the best-selling drug in pharmaceutical history. While Humira has lost exclusivity in the U.S. market facing biosimilar competition, AbbVie has executed one of the most successful product transitions in the industry with its next-generation immunology drugs Skyrizi and Rinvoq, which together are generating revenue that more than offsets Humira's decline. This transition demonstrates AbbVie's research and development prowess and its ability to replenish its pipeline — a critical capability for long-term pharmaceutical investors. Beyond immunology, AbbVie has a growing oncology franchise with drugs like Imbruvica and Venclexta, and its aesthetics portfolio, anchored by Botox, provides exposure to a market that has proven remarkably resilient even during economic downturns. People tend to continue their Botox treatments because they are relatively affordable compared to larger discretionary purchases and have become part of many consumers' regular self-care routines. AbbVie has increased its dividend for over 50 consecutive years, inheriting the streak from Abbott Laboratories, and its current yield typically ranges from 3% to 4%. The company's ability to maintain and grow this dividend through the Humira patent cliff — one of the most anticipated revenue losses in pharmaceutical history — speaks volumes about the strength and diversification of its overall business. During the 2020 pandemic crash, AbbVie's stock recovered to pre-crash levels within months, significantly outpacing the broader market's recovery.

items.name: PepsiCo (PEP)
items.detail: PepsiCo is a global food and beverage giant that offers one of the most compelling defensive profiles in the consumer staples sector. Unlike its primary rival Coca-Cola, which focuses almost exclusively on beverages, PepsiCo has a uniquely diversified portfolio that spans both beverages and snacks, giving it multiple revenue streams and reduced dependence on any single product category. Its beverage brands include Pepsi, Mountain Dew, Gatorade, and Tropicana, while its Frito-Lay division produces iconic snack brands like Lay's, Doritos, Cheetos, and Quaker Oats. This dual-category approach is a significant defensive advantage because snack consumption tends to remain stable or even increase during downturns as people substitute home snacking for more expensive dining-out experiences. During the 2008 recession, PepsiCo's snack volumes actually grew as consumers shifted their food spending toward affordable at-home options. The company's geographic diversification is also a strength — PepsiCo generates nearly half its revenue from international markets, providing a natural hedge against regional economic weakness. PepsiCo's pricing power is another critical defensive attribute. The company has demonstrated the ability to raise prices on its products even during inflationary periods without suffering significant volume declines, a testament to the strength of its brands and consumer loyalty. With 50 consecutive years of dividend increases, PepsiCo is a Dividend King, and its dividend yield typically ranges from 2.5% to 3.5%. The company's free cash flow generation consistently exceeds $8 billion annually, providing ample coverage for the dividend and funding for continued business investment. During the COVID-19 market crash, PepsiCo stock fell less than 20% and recovered to new highs within months, outperforming the broader market significantly.

content.disclaimer: This article is for educational purposes only and does not constitute financial advice. Always conduct your own research and consider consulting a financial advisor before making investment decisions.

thumbnail.alt: Defensive stocks shield protecting portfolio during market downturn

hero.alt: Recession-proof investment strategy with defensive stock picks

mid.alt: Resilient companies performing well in bear markets
